questions.question: What was the name of the ship on which Charles Darwin traveled to make his world-changing observations about natural selection?
questions.explanation: Charles Darwin sailed aboard HMS Beagle from December 1831 to October 1836, on a voyage that took him around the world. He was 22 when he boarded, officially as a gentleman companion for the captain. His observations in South America and the Galapagos Islands provided the foundation for his theory of evolution by natural selection, published 23 years later in On the Origin of Species.
questions.memory_hook: Darwin = HMS Beagle, 1831-1836. He was hired as a companion for the captain, not a scientist. He collected, observed, and took notes for 5 years. Then thought about what he'd seen for 23 more years before publishing. One voyage changed our understanding of life itself.

questions.question: The Soviet Union's attempt to suppress Hungarian revolt in 1956 was led by which Soviet leader?
questions.explanation: Nikita Khrushchev, who succeeded Stalin in 1953, ordered Soviet tanks into Budapest in November 1956 to crush the Hungarian Revolution. Hungarians had risen up against Soviet-imposed communist rule. About 2,500 Hungarians were killed and 200,000 fled as refugees. The same year, Khrushchev gave his famous 'Secret Speech' denouncing Stalin's crimes. The simultaneous Suez Crisis distracted Western attention from Hungary.
questions.memory_hook: Khrushchev = crushed Hungary 1956. Same year he denounced Stalin's crimes in the Secret Speech. He condemned Stalin's methods while using them. The contradiction defined his rule. Eisenhower didn't intervene in Hungary because the Suez Crisis was happening simultaneously.

questions.question: Which ancient Greek city-state was known for its warrior culture and military training from childhood?
questions.explanation: Sparta was an ancient Greek city-state whose society was organized entirely around military excellence. Boys were taken from their families at age 7 and placed in the agoge — an intensive military training program. Spartan women had more rights than other Greek women (they could own property and exercise) because physically fit mothers were needed to raise warriors. Despite their fame, Sparta produced relatively little art, philosophy, or literature.
questions.memory_hook: Sparta = warrior culture from age 7. The agoge was brutal by design — weakness was beaten out, endurance was praised. Sparta was the only Greek city without walls — they said their men were their walls. Ironic that the most militaristic Greek state left the least cultural legacy.

questions.question: The first trans-continental railroad in the United States was completed in which year?
questions.explanation: The First Transcontinental Railroad was completed on May 10, 1869, when the Union Pacific and Central Pacific railroads met at Promontory Summit, Utah. The golden spike ceremony connected the eastern and western United States by rail for the first time. The railroad made cross-country travel practical (weeks instead of months) and was instrumental in settling the American West. It was largely built by Chinese and Irish immigrant laborers.
questions.memory_hook: Transcontinental railroad completed May 10, 1869. Golden spike at Promontory, Utah. Cut cross-country travel from months to days. Built mostly by Chinese immigrants (Central Pacific) and Irish immigrants (Union Pacific). Both groups were later discriminated against by the country they helped connect.

questions.question: In swimming, what is the shortest Olympic race distance?
questions.explanation: The 50-meter freestyle is the shortest swimming event in the Olympics and is considered the sprint of the pool. Swimmers complete one length of the 50-meter pool without turning. The race lasts only about 21-22 seconds for elite male swimmers. In 2009, Cesar Cielo of Brazil set the world record of 20.91 seconds using a controversial full-body polyurethane suit that was subsequently banned.
questions.memory_hook: 50m freestyle = shortest Olympic swim race. About 21 seconds for the world's fastest. One lap, no turns, pure speed. The world record was set in a suit they then banned. If those suits still existed, records would be much faster.

questions.question: Which country dominated Olympic weightlifting for most of the 20th century?
questions.explanation: The Soviet Union dominated Olympic weightlifting throughout the Cold War era, winning the most medals across the sport's history. Soviet weightlifters benefited from state-sponsored training, scientific coaching methods, and (as emerged later) systematic doping programs. After the USSR dissolved, Russia, Ukraine, and other former Soviet republics continued the tradition. Bulgaria also produced world-class weightlifters, particularly in lighter weight classes.
questions.memory_hook: Soviet Union = Olympic weightlifting dominant. State-funded, scientifically trained, systematically enhanced. After the USSR fell, the tradition fragmented across former republics — but Russia, Ukraine, Kazakhstan, and others stayed competitive. The Soviet system essentially invented modern sports science.

questions.question: In which sport do teams of four athletes compete to slide down an icy track in a large sled, steering with their feet?
questions.explanation: Bobsled (or bobsleigh) involves teams of two or four athletes sliding down a narrow, twisted, banked ice track in a gravity-powered sled. Pilots steer the sled using handles connected to the front runners. Four-man bob teams can exceed 150 km/h. Luge is similar but uses a different body position (lying face-up on a small sled); skeleton uses the face-down position.
questions.memory_hook: Bobsled = team in a large sled. Two or four athletes. Over 150 km/h. Luge is face-up, skeleton is face-down, bobsled is sitting up. The 1988 Jamaican bobsled team became famous (and a movie) for competing in a winter sport they had no infrastructure for. They finished, which was enough.

questions.question: Which 2002 film by Steven Spielberg stars Tom Hanks as a man who is stranded in an airport after his country ceases to exist?
questions.explanation: The Terminal (2004, not 2002) starred Tom Hanks as Viktor Navorski, a man from the fictional country of Krakozhia who becomes stranded in JFK Airport when his country undergoes a coup and his passport becomes invalid. The film was loosely inspired by the true story of Mehran Karimi Nasseri, an Iranian refugee who lived in Charles de Gaulle Airport in Paris for 18 years.
questions.memory_hook: The Terminal = Tom Hanks stuck in an airport. Inspired by a real man (Nasseri) who lived in a Paris airport for 18 years. Spielberg and Hanks made a crowd-pleasing film from an absurd but genuine human situation. The real Nasseri was eventually given documents but had grown so accustomed to airport life he kept living there.

questions.question: Which rapper and producer created the 'Chronic' sound that defined West Coast gangsta rap in 1992?
questions.explanation: Dr. Dre released The Chronic in December 1992, defining the G-funk sound — funk-influenced production with synthesized bass lines, laid-back tempos, and the vocal style of his protege Snoop Dogg. The album sold over 3 million copies and launched both Dre's and Snoop's solo careers. Dre went on to produce Eminem's breakthrough albums and founded Beats Electronics, sold to Apple for $3 billion in 2014.
questions.memory_hook: Dr. Dre = The Chronic, 1992. Invented G-funk. Launched Snoop Dogg, then Eminem, then Kendrick Lamar. Sold Beats to Apple for $3 billion. One of the most commercially successful music producers in history, spanning three decades of dominance.

questions.question: The 2000 film 'Gladiator,' starring Russell Crowe, won 5 Academy Awards including Best Picture. Who directed it?
questions.explanation: Ridley Scott directed Gladiator (2000), starring Russell Crowe as Maximus Decimus Meridius, a Roman general enslaved and forced to become a gladiator. The film won 5 Academy Awards including Best Picture and Best Actor (Crowe). It revived the sword-and-sandal epic genre and grossed $460 million worldwide. Ridley Scott also directed Alien, Blade Runner, and Thelma & Louise.
questions.memory_hook: Gladiator = Ridley Scott, 2000. Russell Crowe. 'Are you not entertained?' Best Picture. Scott later directed Thelma & Louise and Blade Runner — completely different genres, same visual mastery. He's one of few directors who's made certified classics in three different genres.

questions.question: Which mountain range runs along the western edge of North America, stretching from Alaska to Mexico?
questions.explanation: The Rocky Mountains stretch approximately 4,800 km from northern British Columbia and Alberta in Canada down through the western United States to New Mexico. The highest peak is Mount Elbert in Colorado at 4,401 meters. The Rockies are the continental divide — water on the west side flows to the Pacific, water on the east flows toward the Atlantic or Gulf of Mexico.
questions.memory_hook: Rocky Mountains = western spine of North America. 4,800 km from Alaska to New Mexico. The Continental Divide runs along the Rockies — rain on one side eventually reaches the Pacific, rain on the other reaches the Gulf of Mexico or Atlantic. One mountain range, two ocean watersheds.

questions.question: What is the name for the region of space where a black hole's gravity is so strong that not even light can escape?
questions.explanation: The event horizon is the boundary around a black hole beyond which nothing — not even light — can escape the black hole's gravitational pull. It is sometimes called the 'point of no return.' Inside the event horizon lies the singularity, where gravity becomes theoretically infinite and current physics breaks down. The first image of a black hole's shadow (and event horizon) was captured in 2019 by the Event Horizon Telescope.
questions.memory_hook: Event horizon = the point of no return around a black hole. Light can't escape if it crosses it. The first image of one was taken in 2019 — the shadow of the black hole in galaxy M87. It took eight Earth-sized telescopes linked together to capture it.
